If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of an F-distribution with given _numerator_ and _denominator degrees of freedom_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

Probability_Commands.name: Gamma
desc: Calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamma_distribution[Gamma distribution] at _variable value v_, i.e. the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a Gamma distribution defined by the parameters _alpha_ and _beta_.

If _Cumulative = true_, calculates the value of the cumulative distribution function of a Gamma distribution with given _alpha_ and _beta_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it calculates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a Gamma distribution with given _alpha_ and _beta_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

Probability_Commands.name: HyperGeometric
syntax: HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
HyperGeometric( <Population Size>, <Number of Successes>, <Sample Size>, <Variable Value>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
desc: Returns a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[Hypergeometric distribution] bar graph.

When _Cumulative = false_ returns a bar graph of a Hypergeometric distribution, otherwise it returns the graph of a cumulative Hypergeometric distribution function. First three parameters are same as above.

Let X be a Hypergeometric random variable and v the variable value. The first three parameters are same as above. * When _Cumulative = false_ it returns P( X = _v_) * When _Cumulative = true_ it returns P( X ≤ _v_) == CAS Syntax In the image:16px-Menu_view_cas.svg.png[Menu view cas.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/CAS_View.adoc[CAS View] you can use only the following syntax:
examples: Parameters: * _Population size_: number of balls in an urn * _Number of Successes_: number of white balls in the urn * _Sample Size_: number of balls drawn from the urn A random sample is repeatedly extracted from an urn, without replacement. HyperGeometric(100, 50, 5) generates a bar graph showing the probability distribution of the number of white balls in the sample.

Assume you select two balls out of ten balls, two of which are white, without putting any back. * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 0, false) yields stem:[\frac{28}{45}], the probability of selecting zero white balls, * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 1, false) yields stem:[\frac{16}{45}], the probability of selecting one white ball, * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 2, false) yields stem:[\frac{1}{45}], the probability of selecting both white balls, * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 3, false) yields _0_, the probability of selecting three white balls. * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 0, true) yields stem:[\frac{28}{45}], the probability of selecting zero (or less) white balls, * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 1, true) yields stem:[\frac{44}{45}], the probability of selecting one or less white balls, * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 2, true) yields _1_, the probability of selecting two or less white balls and * HyperGeometric(10, 2, 2, 3, true) yields _1_, the probability of selecting three or less white balls.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseBeta
desc: Computes the inverse of the Beta cumulative distribution with parameters _α_ and _β_ for a given probability _p_. In other words, the command finds t such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a random variable with a Beta distribution. Probability _p_ is any value in the interval _[0,1]_.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseBinomial
syntax: InverseBinomial( <Number of Trials>, <Probability of Success>, <Cumulative Probability> )
desc: Returns least integer _n_ such that _P(X ≤ n) ≥ p_, where _p_ is the probability and _X_ is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_distribution[binomial random variable] given by _Number of Trials_ and _Probability of Success_.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseBinomialMinimumTrials
syntax: InverseBinomialMinimumTrials(Cumulative Probability, Probability of Success, Number of Successes)
desc: Returns the minimum number _n_ of trials to obtain the given number of successes.
examples: `InverseBinomialMinimumTrials(0.5, 0.2 ,50)` yields 254.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseCauchy
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_distribution[Cauchy distribution] at probability _p_, where the Cauchy distribution is defined by parameters _median_ and _scale_. In other words, finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a Cauchy random variable. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseChiSquared
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi-square_distribution[Chi squared distribution] at probability _p_, where the Chi squared distribution is defined by the given _degrees of freedom_. In other words, it finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a Chi squared random variable. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseExponential
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_distribution[Exponential distribution] at probability _p_, where the Exponential distribution is defined by the given parameter _lambda_. In other words, finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is an Exponential random variable. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseFDistribution
syntax: InverseFDistribution( <Numerator Degrees of Freedom>, <Denominator Degrees of Freedom>, <Probability> )
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of an https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F-distribution[F-distribution] at probability _p_, where the F-distribution is defined by the given _degrees of freedom_. In other words, it finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a random variable with an F-distribution. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseGamma
desc: Computes the inverse of the Gamma cumulative distribution with parameters _α_ and _β_ for a given probability _p_. In other words, the command finds t such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a random variable with a Gamma distribution. Probability _p_ is any value in the interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseHyperGeometric
desc: Returns least integer _n_ such that _P(X ≤ n) ≥ p_, where _p_ is the probability and _X_ is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypergeometric_distribution[hypergeometric random variable] given by _Population Size_, _Number of Successes_ and _Sample Size_.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseLogNormal
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Log-normal_distribution[log-normal distribution] at probability _p_, where the log-normal distribution is defined by the given parameters _mean_ and _standard deviation_. In other words, it finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a log-normal random variable. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [_0, 1_].
examples: * InverseLogNormal(10, 20, 1/3) returns _3.997_. * InverseLogNormal(1000, 2, 1) returns stem:[ \infty ].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseLogistic
desc: Computes the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistic_distribution[Logistic distribution] at probability _p_, where the Logistic distribution is defined by the given parameters _mean_ and _scale_. In other words, it finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a Logistic random variable. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].
examples: InverseLogistic(100, 2, 1) yields _stem:[ \infty ]_.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseNormal
desc: Evaluates the expression stem:[\Phi^{-1}(P) \cdot \sigma + \mu ] at given probability _P_, where stem:[\Phi^{-1}] is the inverse of the cumulative distribution function _Φ_ for _N(0,1)_, defined by the given parameters _mean_ and _standard deviation_.
examples: InverseNormal(50, 2, 0.9) yields 52.56, that is the 90th percentile of a normal distribution with a mean of 50 and standard deviation 2.

Probability_Commands.name: InversePascal
desc: Returns least integer _n_ such that _P(X≤n) ≥ p_, where p is the probability and _X_ is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal random variable] given by _n_ and _p_.

Probability_Commands.name: InversePoisson
desc: Returns the least integer _n_ such that _P(X≤n) ≥ p_, where _p_ is the given _probability_ and _X_ is a Poisson random variable with given _mean_.

Probability_Commands.name: InverseTDistribution
desc: Evaluates at _p_ the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T-distribution[t-distribution] with the given number of _degrees of freedom_. In other words, it finds _r_ such that _P(X≤r)=p_, where X is a random variable with a t-distribution. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseWeibull
desc: Evaluates the inverse of the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weibull_distribution[Weibull distribution] at given _p_, where the Weibull distribution is defined by the given parameters _shape_ and _scale_. In other words, it finds _t_ such that _P(X ≤ t) = p_, where _X_ is a random variable with Weibull distribution. Probability _p_ must be a value in the closed interval [0,1].

Probability_Commands.name: InverseZipf
desc: Returns the least integer _n_ such that _P(X≤n) ≥ p_, where X is a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf_distribution[Zipf random variable] defined by the given _number of elements_ and _exponent_, and _p_ is the probability.

Probability_Commands.name: LogNormal
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution at _variable value_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a log-normal distribution defined by the given parameters _mean_ and _standard deviation_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a log-normal distribution with given _mean_ and _standard deviation_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a log-normal distribution with given _mean_ and _standard deviation_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

Probability_Commands.name: Logistic
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistic_distribution[logistic distribution] at variable value _v_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a logistic distribution defined by the given parameters _mean_ and _scale_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a logistic distribution with given _mean_ and _scale_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a logistic distribution with given _mean_ and _scale_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.

Probability_Commands.name: Normal
syntax: Normal( <Mean>, <Standard Deviation>, <Variable Value u> , <Variable Value v>)
desc: Evaluates the function stem:[\Phi \left(\frac{x- \mu}{\sigma} \right) ] at _variable value v_, where _Φ_ is the cumulative distribution function of _N(0,1)_ with given _mean μ_ and _standard deviation σ_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution[normal distribution] with given _mean_ and _standard deviation_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

Computes the probability of a normal random variable in the interval [_u_, _v_], given the mean and the standard deviation. In other words, the syntax `Normal(_m_, _s_, _u_, _v_)` is equivalent to `Normal(_m_, _s_, _v_, true) - Normal(_m_, _s_, _u_, true)`
examples: Normal(2, 0.5, 1) yields _0.02_ in the ] xref:/Algebra_View.adoc[Algebra View] and stem:[\frac{erf(-\sqrt{2})+1}{2}] in the ] xref:/CAS_View.adoc[CAS View].

Probability_Commands.name: Pascal
desc: Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_binomial_distribution[Pascal distribution]. The Pascal distribution models the number of failures before the __n__th success in repeated mutually independent Bernoulli trials, each with probability of success _p_.
Returns a bar graph of a Pascal distribution when _Cumulative_ = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Pascal distribution when _Cumulative_ = true. First two parameters are same as above.
Let X be a Pascal random variable and _v_ the variable value. Returns P( X = _v_) when _Cumulative_ = false. Returns P( X ≤ _v_) when _Cumulative_ = true. First two parameters are same as above.
examples: If the number of independent Bernoulli trials that must be successful is n = 1, the probability of success in one trial is p = stem:[\frac{1}{6}], then the probability of 2 failures before the success is given by Pascal(1, 1/6, 2, false) which yields _0.12_ in the view algebra.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Algebra_View.adoc[Algebra View] and _25/216_ in the view cas.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/CAS_View.adoc[CAS View].

Probability_Commands.name: Poisson
desc: Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poisson_distribution[Poisson distribution] with given mean _λ_.
Returns a bar graph of a Poisson distribution when _Cumulative = false_. Returns a graph of a cumulative Poisson distribution when _Cumulative = true_. The first parameter is same as above.
Let X be a Poisson random variable. Returns P( X = _v_) when _Cumulative_ = false. Returns P( X ≤ _v_) when _Cumulative_ = true. First parameter is same as above.
examples: * Poisson(3, 1, true) yields _0.2_ in the view algebra.svg,width=16,height=16] _Algebra View_ and _stem:[\frac{4}{e³}] in the view cas.svg,width=16,height=16]_ CAS View__.__ * Poisson(3, 1, false) yields _0.15_ in the view algebra.svg,width=16,height=16] _Algebra View_ and _stem:[\frac{3}{e³}]_ in the view cas.svg,width=16,height=16] _CAS View_.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomBetween
desc: Generates a random integer between _minimum_ and _maximum_ (inclusive).
If _Boolean Fixed_ = "true", it generates a random integer between _minimum_ and _maximum_ (inclusive), which is updated just once (when file is loaded and also on undo/redo).
Generates a list of random integers between _minimum_ and _maximum_ (inclusive). The number of random integers in the list is the _number of samples_.
examples: RandomBetween(0, 10) yields a number between _0_ and _10_ (inclusive)
RandomBetween(0, 10, true) yields a number between _0_ and _10_ (inclusive)

Probability_Commands.name: RandomBinomial
desc: Generates a random number from a binomial distribution with _n_ trials and probability _p_.
examples: RandomBinomial(3, 0.1) gives _j ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3}_, where the probability of getting _j_ is the probability of an event with probability _0.1_ occurring _j_ times in three tries.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomDiscrete
desc: Returns a random number from the first list according to the (relative) probability distribution defined in the second list. The two lists must have the same length, and the sum of values in the second list may not be 1, since the probabilities are normalized.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomNormal
desc: Generates a random number from a normal distribution with given mean and standard deviation.
examples: RandomNormal(3, 0.1) yields a random value from a normal distribution with a mean of _3_ and standard deviation of _0.1_.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomPointIn
desc: Creates a random point inside a given polygon or closed conic.
Returns a random point inside the polygon with given vertices.
Creates a random point with x-coordinate from interval [xMin,xMax] and y-coordinate from interval [yMin, yMax].
examples: RandomPointIn(Polygon(A,B,C)) and RandomPointIn(A,B,C) both give random point inside triangle ABC. To get a random point that belongs to the list use xref:/commands/RandomElement.adoc[Random Element] instead.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomPoisson
desc: Generates a random number from a Poisson distribution with given mean.
examples: RandomPoisson(3) yields a random value from a Poisson distribution with a mean of _3_.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomPolynomial
desc: Returns a randomly generated polynomial in _x_ of degree _d_, whose (integer) coefficients are in the range from _minimum_ to _maximum_, both included. == CAS Syntax The following command is only available in the image:16px-Menu_view_cas.svg.png[Menu view cas.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/CAS_View.adoc[CAS View].
Returns a randomly generated polynomial in _Variable_ of degree _d_, whose (integer) coefficients are in the range from _minimum_ to _maximum_, both included.
examples: * RandomPolynomial(0, 1, 2) yields either _1_ or _2_. * RandomPolynomial(2, 1, 2) yields a random polynomial with a degree of two and only _1_ and _2_ as coefficients, for example _2x^2^ + x + 1_.

* RandomPolynomial(a, 0, 1, 2) yields either _1_ or _2_. * RandomPolynomial(a, 2, 1, 2) yields a random polynomial with a degree of two and only _1_ and _2_ as coefficients, for example _2a^2^ + a + 1_.

Probability_Commands.name: RandomUniform
desc: Returns random real number from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_distribution_(continuous)[uniform distribution] on interval [_min_, _max_].
Returns a list of _n_ random real numbers from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_distribution_(continuous)[uniform distribution] on interval [_min_, _max_].
examples: RandomUniform(0, 1) returns a random number between _0_ and _1_
RandomUniform(0, 1, 3) returns a list of three random numbers between _0_ and _1_

Probability_Commands.name: TDistribution
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution at _variable value v_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a t-distribution with the given _degrees of freedom_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a t-distribution with given _degrees of freedom_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Student%27s_t-distribution[t-distribution] with given _degrees of freedom_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution. == CAS Syntax
examples: TDistribution(10, 0) yields _0.5_.

Probability_Commands.name: Triangular
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a triangular distribution at _variable value v_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a triangular distribution in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_] with the given _mode_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triangular_distribution[triangular distribution] with given _mode_ at the given _variable value_ in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_], otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative_ is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a triangular distribution with given _mode_ in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_], otherwise it creates the probability density function of a triangular distribution with given _mode_ in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_].
examples: Triangular(0, 5, 2, 2) yields _0.4_.

Probability_Commands.name: Uniform
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a uniform distribution at _variable value v_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a uniform distribution in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_].

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniform_distribution_(continuous)[uniform distribution] at the given _variable value_ in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_], otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative_ is true, creates the cumulative distribution function of a uniform distribution in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_], otherwise it creates the probability density function of a uniform distribution in [_lower bound_, _upper bound_].

Probability_Commands.name: Weibull
desc: Evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a Weibull distribution at _variable value v_, i.e. calculates the probability _P(X ≤ v)_ where _X_ is a random variable with a Weibull distribution defined by the given parameters _shape_ and _scale_.

If _Cumulative = true_, evaluates the cumulative distribution function of a Weibull distribution with given _shape_ and _scale_ at the given _variable value_, otherwise it evaluates the probability density function of the distribution at _variable value_.

If _Cumulative = true_, creates the cumulative density function (cdf) of a Weibull distribution with given _shape_ and _scale_, otherwise it creates the probability density function (pdf) of the distribution.
examples: * Weibull(0.5, 1, 0) yields _0_. * Weibull(0.5, 1, 1) yields _stem:[1 - \frac{1} { e } ]._

Probability_Commands.name: Zipf
syntax: Zipf( <Number of Elements>, <Exponent> , <Variable Value v>, <Boolean Cumulative> )
desc: Returns a bar graph of a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf%27s_law[Zipf distribution]. Parameters: _Number of Elements_: number of elements whose rank we study _Exponent_: exponent characterizing the distribution
Returns a bar graph of a _Zipf distribution_ when _Cumulative_ = false. Returns a graph of a cumulative Zipf distribution when _Cumulative_ = true. First two parameters are same as above.
Let X be a Zipf random variable. Returns P( X = _v_) when _Cumulative_ = false. Returns P( X ≤ _v_) when _Cumulative_ = true. First two parameters are same as above.
examples: Zipf(10, 1 , 5, false) yields _0.07_ in the view algebra.svg,width=16,height=16] _Algebra View_ and _stem:[\frac{504}{7381}]_ in the view cas.svg,width=16,height=16] _CAS View_.

3D_Commands.name: Bottom
desc: Creates the bottom of the limited quadric.
examples: Bottom(cylinder) yields a circle.

3D_Commands.name: Cone
desc: Creates a cone with given base and height.
Creates a cone with vertex (second point), circle center (first point) and given radius.
Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to the given vector and apex angle 2α.

3D_Commands.name: Cube
desc: Creates a cube having as base the given square.

Creates a cube having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are uniquely determined by the given direction, that should be one of: * a vector, a segment, a line, a ray *orthogonal* to the segment, or * a polygon, a plane *parallel* to the segment. + The created cube will have: * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
Creates a cube with three (adjacent) points of the first face. The points have to start a square for the cube to be defined.

3D_Commands.name: Cylinder
desc: Creates a cylinder with given base and given height.
Creates a cylinder with given radius and with given points as the centers of the top and bottom.

3D_Commands.name: Dodecahedron
desc: Creates a dodecahedron having the given regular pentagon as base.

Creates a dodecahedron having the segment between two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: * a vector, a segment, a line, a ray *orthogonal* to the segment, or * a polygon, a plane *parallel* to the segment. + The created dodecahedron will have: * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
Creates a dodecahedron with three (adjacent) points of the first face. The points have to start a regular pentagon for the dodecahedron to be defined.

3D_Commands.name: Ends
desc: Creates the top and the bottom of the limited quadric.
examples: * Ends( cylinder ) yields two circles. * Ends( cone ) yields a circle and the cone end (point).

3D_Commands.name: Height
desc: Calculates the "oriented" height of the given solid.
examples: * Height( <Cone> ) calculates the "oriented" height of the given cone. * Height( <Cylinder> ) calculates the "oriented" height of the given cylinder. * Height( <Polyhedron> ) calculates the "oriented" height of the given solid polyhedron.

3D_Commands.name: Icosahedron
desc: Creates an icosahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.

Creates an icosahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: * a vector, a segment, a line, a ray *orthogonal* to the segment, or * a polygon, a plane *parallel* to the segment. + The created icosahedron will have: * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
Creates an icosahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the icosahedron to be defined.

3D_Commands.name: InfiniteCone
desc: Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to the given vector and apex angle _2α_.
Creates an infinite cone with given first point as vertex, line through two points as axis of symmetry and apex angle _2α_.
Creates an infinite cone with given point as vertex, axis of symmetry parallel to given line and apex angle _2α_.

3D_Commands.name: InfiniteCylinder
desc: Creates an infinite cylinder with given radius and given line as an axis of symmetry.
Creates an infinite cylinder with given radius and with axis of symmetry through a given point parallel to the vector.
Creates an infinite cylinder with given radius and with line through two points as an axis of symmetry.
examples: InfiniteCylinder( xAxis, 2 ) creates an infinite cylinder with radius 2 and with the x-axis as axis of symmetry.

3D_Commands.name: IntersectConic
desc: Intersects the plane with the quadric.
Returns a conic defined in case where the intersection is actually a conic.
examples: IntersectConic(sphere1, sphere2) creates the intersection conic of two spheres.

3D_Commands.name: Net
desc: Creates the net of a convex polyhedron, on the plane containing the face used for its construction. The number is used to define the progress of the unfolding procedure, and needs to be between 0 and 1. The net is totally unfold when the given number is 1.
Applicable only to cubes (for the moment), allows you to create different nets of a cube, by specifying the face and edges that need to be cut to create the net.

3D_Commands.name: Octahedron
desc: Creates an octahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.

Creates an octahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: * a vector, a segment, a line, a ray *orthogonal* to the segment, or * a polygon, a plane *parallel* to the segment. + The created octahedron will have: * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
Creates an octahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the octahedron to be defined.

3D_Commands.name: PerpendicularPlane
desc: Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given line.
Creates a plane through the given point, perpendicular to the given vector.

3D_Commands.name: Plane
desc: Creates the plane containing the given polygon.
Creates the plane containing the conic.
Creates the plane through the given point, parallel to the given plane.

3D_Commands.name: PlaneBisector
desc: Creates the plane orthogonal bisector between the two points.
Creates the plane orthogonal bisector of the segment.

3D_Commands.name: Prism
desc: Returns a prism defined by the given points.
Creates a prism with the given polygon as base and the point as first top point.
Creates a right prism with the polygon as base and given height.
examples: Prism(A, B, C, D) creates the prism with base ABC and top DEF. The vectors AD, BE, CF are equal.
Prism(poly1, A) creates a prism with base _poly1_ and top point _A_.

3D_Commands.name: Pyramid
desc: Returns a pyramid defined by the given points.
Creates a pyramid with the given polygon as base and the point as apex.
Returns a centered pyramid defined by the polygon as base and given height.
examples: Pyramid(A, B, C, D) creates the pyramid with base _ABC_ and apex _D_.
Pyramid(poly1, A) creates a pyramid with base _poly1_ and apex _A_.

3D_Commands.name: Side
desc: Creates the side of the limited quadric.
examples: Side( cylinder ) creates the curved surface area of the cylinder.

3D_Commands.name: Sphere
desc: Creates a sphere with center and radius.
Creates a sphere with center in the first point through the second point.
examples: Sphere((0, 0, 0), (1, 1, 1)) yields _x² + y² + z² = 3_

3D_Commands.name: Surface
syntax: Surface( <Expression>, <Expression>, <Expression>, <Parameter Variable 1>, <Start Value>, <End Value>, <Parameter Variable 2>, <Start Value>, <End Value> )
desc: Yields the Cartesian parametric 3D surface for the given _x_-expression (first _<Expression>_), _y_-expression (second _<Expression>_) and _z_-expression (third _<Expression>_), using two _<Parameter Variables>_ within the given intervals
Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the given _Function_ from 0 to given _Angle_ around the x-axis.
Creates a surface of revolution, rotating the _Curve_ from 0 to given _Angle_ around the _Line_.
examples: Let _r_ and _R_ be two positive real numbers: Surface((R + r cos( u)) cos(v) , (R + r cos( u)) sin(v) , r sin(u ), u, 0, 2 π , v, 0, 2 π) creates the torus generated by a circle of radius _r_ whose center rotates about zAxis at a distance _R_.

3D_Commands.name: Tetrahedron
desc: Creates a tetrahedron having as base the given equilateral triangle.

Creates a tetrahedron having the segment between the two points as an edge. The other vertices are univocally determined by the given direction, that needs to be: * a vector, a segment, a line, a ray *orthogonal* to the segment, or * a polygon, a plane *parallel* to the segment. The created tetrahedron will have: * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane orthogonal to the given vector/segment/line/ray, or * a face with the segment as an edge in a plane parallel to the polygon/plane.
Creates a tetrahedron with the three points of the first face. The points have to draw an equilateral triangle for the tetrahedron to be defined.

3D_Commands.name: Top
desc: Creates the top of the limited quadric.
examples: * Top( cylinder ) yields a circle. * Top( cone ) yields the cone end (point).

3D_Commands.name: Volume
desc: Calculates the volume of the given solid.
examples: * Volume( <Pyramid> ) calculates the volume of the given pyramid. * Volume( <Prism> ) calculates the volume of the given prism. * Volume( <Cone> ) calculates the volume of the given cone. * Volume( <Cylinder> ) calculates the volume of the given cylinder.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: Cell
desc: Returns copy of xref:/Spreadsheet_View.adoc[spreadsheet] cell in given column and row.
examples: Cell(2, 1) returns copy of B1.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: CellRange
desc: Creates a list containing the cell values in this cell range.
examples: Let A1 = 1, A2 = 4, A3 = 9 be xref:/Spreadsheet_View.adoc[spreadsheet] cells values.Then CellRange(A1, A3) returns the list {1, 4, 9}.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: Column
desc: Returns the column of the cell as a number (starting at 1).
examples: q = Column(B3) returns _q = 2_ since column _B_ is the second column of the xref:/Spreadsheet_View.adoc[spreadsheet].

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: ColumnName
desc: Returns the column name of the cell as a text.
examples: r = ColumnName(A1) creates _r = A_ and shows such text - A - in the view graphics.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Graphics_View.adoc[Graphics View].

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: FillCells
desc: Copies the value/equation etc. of the object to the given cellrange. Resulting cells are xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[free objects], i.e. independent of object.
Copies values from the list to the first cells on the right of the given cell. Resulting cells are ([Free, Dependent and Auxiliary Objects|free objects)], i.e. independent of the list.

Copies values from the matrix into the spreadsheet. The upper left corner of the matrix is matched to the given cell. Resulting cells are xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[free objects], i.e. independent of the matrix.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: FillColumn
desc: Copies values from the list to the first cells of the column given by number (1 for A, 2 for B, etc.). Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: FillRow
desc: Copies values from the list to the first cells of the row given by number. Resulting cells are free objects, i.e. independent of the list.

Spreadsheet_Commands.name: Row
desc: Returns the row number of the xref:/Spreadsheet_View.adoc[spreadsheet] cell (starting at 1).
examples: r = Row(B3) yields _r = 3_.

Text_Commands.name: ContingencyTable
syntax: ContingencyTable( <List of Row Values>, <List of Column Values>, <Frequency Table> )
desc: Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists. Unique values from the first list are used as row values in the table. Unique values from the second list are used as column values in the table.

Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] created from the two given lists as described above. The text _Options_ controls the display of optional calculations within the table.
Draws a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingency_table[Contingency Table] using the given list of row values, column values and corresponding frequency table.
examples: ContingencyTable({"Males","Females"},{"Right-handed", "Left-handed"},{{43,9},{44,4}}) yields the corresponding Contingency Table.

Text_Commands.name: ContinuedFraction
desc: Creates the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continued_fraction[continued fraction] approximating a given number. The result is a LaTeX xref:/Texts.adoc[text object]. The fraction is computed numerically within precision 10^-8^.

Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. The number of quotients is less than or equal to _Level_, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^.

Creates the continued fraction approximating the given number. If the parameter _Level_ is specified, the number of quotients is less than or equal to _Level_, but never exceeding the number of quotients needed to achieve the numerical precision of 10^-8^. When _Shorthand_ is _true_, the LaTeX text uses a shorter syntax, and contains a list of the integer parts of the continued fraction.
examples: ContinuedFraction(5.45) gives _stem:[5 + \frac{1}{ 2+ \frac{1}{4+ \frac{1}{ 1+ \frac{1}{ 1 } } } }]_
ContinuedFraction(5.45, 3) gives _stem:[5 + \frac{1}{ 2+ \frac{1}{4+ ... } }]_

Text_Commands.name: First
desc: Gives a new list that contains the first element of the given list.
Gives a new list that contains just the first _n_ elements of the given list.
Gives first character of the text.
examples: First({1, 4, 3}) yields _{1}_.

Text_Commands.name: FormulaText
syntax: FormulaText( <Object>, <Boolean for Substitution of Variables>, <Boolean Show Name> )
desc: Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. Values are substituted for variables by default.
Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (_true_) or if variable names are shown in the text (_false_).

Returns the formula that defines the object as a LaTeX text. The first Boolean variable determines if values are substituted for variables (_true_) or if variable names are shown in the text (_false_), the second Boolean variable determines if the object name is shown in the text (_true_) or not (_false_).
examples: Let _a = 2_ and _f(x) = a x^2^_. FormulaText(f) returns _2 x^2^_ (as a LaTeX text).
Let _a = 2_ and _f(x) = a x^2^_. FormulaText(f, true) returns _2 x^2^_ (as a LaTeX text). FormulaText(f, false) returns _a x^2^_ (as a LaTeX text).

Text_Commands.name: FractionText
desc: Creates and shows in the image:16px-Menu_view_graphics.svg.png[Menu view graphics.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Graphics_View.adoc[Graphics View] a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number.

Creates and shows in the image:16px-Menu_view_graphics.svg.png[Menu view graphics.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Graphics_View.adoc[Graphics View] a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the coordinates of the given point.

Creates and shows in the image:16px-Menu_view_graphics.svg.png[Menu view graphics.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Graphics_View.adoc[Graphics View] a LaTeX text containing the fraction form of the given number. The boolean sets the position of a possible negative sign of the fraction. If _true_, the minus sign will be displayed in the numerator, if _false_ the minus sign will be displayed in front of the fraction.
examples: Given line _a: y = 1.5 x + 2_, FractionText(Slope(a)) creates the LaTeX text stem:[\frac{3}{2}].
Given point _A=(1.33,0.8)_, FractionText(A) creates the LaTeX text _stem:[ \left( \frac{133}{100} ,\frac{4}{5} \right) ]_.

Text_Commands.name: FrequencyTable
syntax: FrequencyTable( <List of Class Boundaries>, <List of Raw Data> )
desc: Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains sorted list of unique elements of list _L_ and second column contains the count of the occurrences of value in the first column. List _L_ can be numbers or text.

If _Cumulative = false_, returns the same table as Frequency( <List of Raw Data> ) If _Cumulative = true_, returns a table whose first column is the same as in FrequencyTable( <List of Raw Data> ) and the second contains cumulative frequencies of values in the first column.

Returns a table (as text) whose first column contains intervals (classes) and second column contains the count of numbers in _List of Raw Data_, which belong to the interval in the first column. All intervals except the highest interval are of the form [a, b). The highest interval has the form [a, b].

Text_Commands.name: IndexOf
desc: Returns position of first occurrence of Object in List.
Same as above, but the search starts at given index.
Specifies the position at which the short text appears for the first time in the whole text.
examples: IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}) returns _3_.
* IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 3) returns _3_. * IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 4) returns _5_. * IndexOf(5, {1, 3, 5, 2, 5, 4}, 6) returns _undefined_.

Text_Commands.name: Last
desc: Gives a new list that contains the last element of the initial list.
Gives a new list that contains just the last _n_ elements of the initial list.
Gives last character of the text.
examples: Last({1, 4, 3}) yields _{3}_.

Text_Commands.name: LetterToUnicode
desc: Converts a single letter into the corresponding https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unicode[Unicode number].
examples: LetterToUnicode("a") returns the number 97.

Text_Commands.name: Ordinal
desc: Turns a number into an ordinal (as a text).
examples: Ordinal(5) returns "5th".

Text_Commands.name: ParseToFunction
desc: Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding xref:/Functions.adoc[function].
Parses the string and stores the result to a xref:/Functions.adoc[function] _f_, which must be defined and xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[free] before the command is used.
Parses the text containing the function definition and creates the corresponding xref:/Functions.adoc[function] of the variables defined in the list.
examples: * ParseToFunction("x^2") creates the function _f_(_x_) = __x__^2^_._ * ParseToFunction("t+2/t") creates the function _f_(_t_) = _t_ + 2/_t_.
Define f(x) = 3x² + 2 and text1 = "f(x) = 3x + 1". ParseToFunction(f, text1) returns _f(x) = 3x +1_.

Text_Commands.name: ParseToNumber
desc: Parses the text and stores the result to a xref:/Numbers_and_Angles.adoc[number] _a_, which must be defined and xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[free] before the command is used.
Parses the text and stores the result to a xref:/Numbers_and_Angles.adoc[number].
examples: Define a = 3 and text1 = "6". ParseToNumber(a, text1) returns _a = 6_.
ParseToNumber("1+2+5-pi") creates the number _a = 4.86_.

Text_Commands.name: ReadText
desc: Tells the screen reader to read given text immediately.

Text_Commands.name: ReplaceAll
desc: Creates a new text containing the given _text_ whose _text to match_ has been replaced with the given _text to replace_.
examples: ReplaceAll("3cos(t)+cos(2y)", "cos", "sin") creates the new text "3sin(t)+sin(2y)".
FormulaText(ReplaceAll("3cos(t)+cos(2y)", "cos", "sin")) creates the new LaTeX text "3sin(t)+sin(2y)".

Text_Commands.name: RotateText
desc: Creates a new xref:/LaTeX.adoc[LaTeX] text, rotated by the given angle.
examples: * RotateText("a = 5", 45°) * If you want to place the text "GeoGebra", rotated by 42°, at point (6,6), use the command Text(RotateText("GeoGebra", 42°), (6, 6),true,true)

Text_Commands.name: ScientificText
desc: Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation].
Creates a text displaying the given number in https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation[scientific notation], rounded to the number of significant digits specified by *precision*.
examples: ScientificText(0.002) gives _2 × 10^-3^._
ScientificText(e,5) gives _2.7183 × 10^0^._

Text_Commands.name: Simplify
desc: Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible.
Attempts to tidy up text expressions by removing repeated negatives etc. == CAS Syntax
Simplifies the terms of the given function, if possible. Undefined variables can be included in the terms.
examples: Simplify(x + x + x) yields the function _f(x) = 3x_.
For _a_ = _b_ = _c_ = -1 Simplify("f(x) = " + a + "x² + " + b + "x + " + c) yields the text _f(x) = -x^2^ - x - 1_.

Text_Commands.name: Split
syntax: Split( <Text>, <List of Texts to split on>)
desc: Creates the list of texts obtained by splitting the given text at the given separators (not included in the list).
examples: Split("3cos(t)cos(2y)", {"cos"}) returns {"3", "(t)", "(2y)"}.

Text_Commands.name: SurdText
desc: Creates a text representation of the point, with coordinates in the form stem:[\frac{a+b\sqrt{c}}{d}].
Creates a text representation of the number in the form stem:[\frac{a+b\sqrt{c}}{d}].
Creates a text representation of the number, rewritten as a multiple of the constants in the list. If the list is empty, the command uses a list of common constants.
examples: * SurdText(2.414213562373095) creates the text _stem:[1 + \sqrt{2}]_ * SurdText(2.439230484541326) creates the text _stem:[\frac{7+3 \sqrt{3} }{5}]_

Text_Commands.name: TableText
desc: Creates a text that contains a table of the list objects.

Creates a text that contains a table of the list objects. The optional text “Alignment of text” controls the orientation and alignment of the table text, as well as the alignment of the separator in decimal values.
Creates a text that contains a table of the list objects, with given alignment and cell width (in pixels).
examples: * TableText({x^2, 4}, {x^3, 8}, {x^4, 16}) creates a table as a text object with three rows and two columns. All items of the table are left aligned. * TableText(Sequence(i^2, i, 1, 10)) creates a table as a text object with one row. All items of the table are left aligned.

* TableText({1, 2, 3, 4}, {1, 4, 9, 16}, "v") creates a text with two columns and four rows whose elements are left aligned. * TableText({1, 2, 3, 4}, {1, 4, 9, 16}, "h") creates a text with two rows and four columns whose elements are left aligned. * TableText({11.2, 123.1, 32423.9, "234.0"}, "vr") creates a text with one column whose elements are right aligned. * TableText({A1:A10, B1:B10, C1:C10}, "vl") creates a text with three columns whose elements (left aligned) are the objects in the given _Spreadsheet_ cells. * TableText({{2011.56, 2, 3.7, 4}, {1, 4.2, 9, 16.365}}, "v.") creates a text whose elements are aligned on decimal points ] * TableText({{2011.56, 2, 3.7, 4}, {1, 4.2, 9, 16.365}}, "v%") creates a text whose elements are converted to a percentage, and aligned on decimal points ]

Text_Commands.name: Take
desc: Returns a list containing the elements from _Start Position_ to the end of the initial list.
Returns a text containing the elements from _Start Position_ to the end of the initial text.
Returns a list containing the elements from _Start Position_ to _End Position_ of the initial list.
examples: Take("GeoGebra", 3) yields the text _oGebra_.

Text_Commands.name: Text
desc: Creates a xref:/Texts.adoc[text] containing the formula of the given object.
Creates a text containing the formula of the given object. The Boolean variable determines whether values are substituted to variables (_true_) or variable names are shown in the text (_false_).
Creates a text containing the formula of the given object at the given position (point).
examples: If _a = 2_ and _c = a^2^_, then Text(c) creates the text _"4"_.
If _a = 2_ and _c = a^2^_, then * Text(c, true) creates the text _"4"_ and * Text(c, false) creates the text _"a^2^"_.

Text_Commands.name: TextToUnicode
desc: Turns the text into a list of Unicode numbers, one for each character.
examples: * TextToUnicode("Some text") gives you the list of Unicode numbers _{83, 111, 109, 101, 32, 116, 101, 120, 116}_. * If _text1_ is _"hello"_, then TextToUnicode(text1) gives you the list of Unicode numbers _{104, 101, 108, 108, 111}_.

Text_Commands.name: UnicodeToLetter
desc: Converts the integer Unicode number back into a letter which is displayed as a text object in the image:16px-Menu_view_graphics.svg.png[Menu view graphics.svg,width=16,height=16] xref:/Graphics_View.adoc[Graphics View].
examples: UnicodeToLetter(97) yields the text _"a"_.

Text_Commands.name: UnicodeToText
desc: Converts the integer Unicode numbers back into text.
examples: UnicodeToText({104, 101, 108, 108, 111}) yields the text _"hello"_.

Text_Commands.name: VerticalText
desc: Creates a xref:/LaTeX.adoc[LaTeX] xref:/Texts.adoc[text], containing the given text rotated 90° counter-clockwise.
Creates at the given point a xref:/LaTeX.adoc[LaTeX] xref:/Texts.adoc[text], containing the given text rotated 90° counter-clockwise.
examples: * VerticalText("a = 5") creates the LaTeX text "_a_ = 5" displayed vertically, with its top left corner at (0,0)
* VerticalText("GeoGebra", (6, 6)) creates the LaTeX text "_GeoGebra_" displayed vertically, with its top left corner at (6,6)

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: ConvexHull
desc: Creates https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/convex_hull[convex hull] of given set of points. Returned object is a xref:/commands/Locus.adoc[locus], so it is xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[auxiliary].

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: DelaunayTriangulation
desc: Creates a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delaunay_Triangulation[Delaunay Triangulation] of the list of points. Returned object is a xref:/commands/Locus.adoc[locus], so it is xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[auxiliary].

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: MinimumSpanningTree
desc: Returns the minimum spanning tree of a complete graph on given vertices in which weight of edge _(u,v)_ is the Euclidian distance between _u_ and _v_. The resulting object is a xref:./Locus.adoc[locus].

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: ShortestDistance
syntax: ShortestDistance( <List of Segments>, <Start Point>, <End Point>, <Boolean Weighted> )
desc: Finds shortest path between start point and endpoint in a https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_(mathematics)[graph] given by list of segments. If weighted is false, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weighted_graph#Weighted_graphs_and_networks[weight] of each edge is supposed to be 1 (i.e. we are looking for the path with least number of edges), otherwise it is the length of given segment (we are looking for the geometrically shortest path).

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: TravelingSalesman
desc: Returns the shortest closed path which goes through each point exactly once. Returned object is a xref:/commands/Locus.adoc[locus], so it is xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[auxiliary].

Discrete_Math_Commands.name: Voronoi
desc: Draws the https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voronoi_diagram[Voronoi diagram] for given list of points. Returned object is a xref:/commands/Locus.adoc[locus], so it is xref:/Free_Dependent_and_Auxiliary_Objects.adoc[auxiliary].

Logic_Commands.name: CountIf
desc: Counts the number of elements in the list satisfying the condition.
As above, using a more flexible syntax.
examples: * CountIf(x < 3, {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}) gives you the number _2_. * CountIf(x < 3, A1:A10), where _A1:A10_ is a range of cells in the spreadsheet, counts all cells whose values are less than _3_.

Given points _P_, _Q_, _R_ CountIf(x(A) < 3, A, {P, Q, R}) will count only the points whose x-coordinate is less than _3_. The variable _A_ is replaced in turn with _P_ then _Q_ then _R_ for the check. Therefore CountIf(x(A) < 3, A, {(0, 1), (4, 2), (2, 2)}) yields the number _2_.

Logic_Commands.name: If
desc: Yields a copy of the object _Then_ if the condition evaluates to _true_, and an undefined object if it evaluates to _false_.
Yields a copy of object _Then_ if the condition evaluates to _true_, and a copy of object _Else_ if it evaluates to _false_. *Both objects _must_ be of the same type.*

Yields a copy of "Then 1" when first condition is satisfied, "Then 2" if second condition is satisfied etc. If none of the conditions are satisfied and Else is given, this command yields a copy of Else. Otherwise undefined is returned. == Conditional Functions The *If* command can be used to create conditional functions. Such conditional functions may be used as arguments in any command that takes a function argument, such as xref:/commands/Derivative.adoc[Derivative], xref:/commands/Integral.adoc[Integral], and xref:/commands/Intersect.adoc[Intersect]. == Multivariate Conditional Functions The *If* command can also be used to create multivariate conditional functions. In this case, the definition of the pieces of the given function must contain all the variables of the given function. == If Command in Scripting *If* command can be used in scripts to perform different actions under certain conditions.
examples: * Let _n_ = 3. If(n==3, x + y = 4) yields line _x_ + _y_ = 4, because the condition on number _n_ is met. * Let _n_ = 4. If(n==3, x + y = 4) creates an _undefined_ object, because the condition on number _n_ is not met .
Let _n_ be a number. If(n==3, x + y = 4, x - y = 4) yields line _x_ + _y_ = 4 when _n_ = 3, and line _x_ - _y_ = 4 for all _n_ not equal to 3.

Logic_Commands.name: IsDefined
desc: Returns _true_ or _false_ depending on whether the object is defined or not.
examples: IsDefined(Circle((1,1), -2)) returns _false_.

Logic_Commands.name: IsFactored
desc: Returns ''true'' if the polynomial is factored in stem:[\mathbb Q] and ''false'' otherwise. In general, in order to consider a polynomial decomposition as factored, the coefficient of the leading term of each factor needs to be positive.
examples: * `IsFactored(x)` yields _true_ + * `IsFactored(0.5)` yields _true_ + * `IsFactored(5)` yields _true_ + * `IsFactored(x^2-1)` yields _false_ + * `IsFactored(x^2-2)` yields _true_ + * `IsFactored(x(x+1))` yields _true_ + * `IsFactored(x(2x+2))` yields _false_ + * `IsFactored(x^3-1)` yields _false_ + * `IsFactored(x(x/2+1/2))` yields _false_ + * `+IsFactored((x+1)(x^2-1))+` yields _false_ + * `IsFactored(-2x-2)` yields _false_ + * `IsFactored(2x+2)` yields _false_ +

Logic_Commands.name: IsInRegion
desc: Returns _true_ if the point is in given xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[region] and _false_ otherwise.
examples: IsInRegion((1,2), Polygon((0,0), (2,0), (1,3))) returns _true_.

Logic_Commands.name: IsInteger
desc: Returns _true_ or _false_ depending whether the number is an integer or not.
examples: IsInteger(972 / 9) returns _true_.

Logic_Commands.name: IsPrime
desc: Gives _true_ or _false_ depending on whether the number is prime or not. == CAS Syntax
Gives _true_ or _false_ depending on whether the number is prime or not.
examples: * IsPrime(10) yields _false_, * IsPrime(11) yields _true_.
* IsPrime(10) yields _false_, * IsPrime(11) yields _true_.

Logic_Commands.name: IsTangent
desc: Decides if the line is tangent to the conic. Normally this command computes the result numerically. This behavior can be changed by using the xref:/commands/Prove.adoc[Prove] command.
examples: IsTangent(Line((0,0),(1,0)),Circle((0,1),1)) yields _true_.

Logic_Commands.name: IsVertexForm
desc: Checks if a function is written in vertex form.
examples: IsVertexForm((x+2/3)^2-(2/3)^2) yields _true_
IsVertexForm(2*(3 x-2)^(2)+1) yields _false_

Logic_Commands.name: KeepIf
desc: Creates a new list that only contains those elements of the initial list that fulfil the condition.
This syntax allows a more flexible condition.
examples: KeepIf(x<3, {1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 5, 6}) returns the new list _{1, 2, 1}_.

For Points _P, Q, R_ KeepIf(x(A) < 3, A, {P, Q, R}) will filter the points whose _x_-coordinate is less than _3_ out of the list. The variable _A_ is replaced in turn with _P_ then _Q_ then _R_ for the check.

Logic_Commands.name: Relation
desc: Shows a message box that gives you information about the relation between two or more (up to 4) objects.

Shows a message box that gives you information about the relation between two objects. This command allows you to find out whether * two lines are perpendicular * two lines are parallel * two (or more) objects are equal * a point lies on a line or conic * a line is tangent or a passing line to a conic * three points are collinear * three lines are concurrent (or parallel) * four points are concyclic (or collinear). Some of these checks can also be performed symbolically. If GeoGebra supports symbolic check for a certain property, the "More" button appears. By clicking it, GeoGebra may provide more information whether the property is true in general (eventually under certain conditions).

Transformation_Commands.name: Dilate
desc: Dilates the object from a point of origin using the given factor.
Dilates the object from a point, which is the dilation center point, using the given factor.

Transformation_Commands.name: Reflect
desc: Reflects the geometric object through a given point.
Reflects an object (e.g. an image) across a given line.
Inverts the geometric object with respect to a circle.

Transformation_Commands.name: Rotate
desc: Rotates the xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[geometric object] by the angle around the axis origin.
Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given point.
Rotates the geometric object by the angle around the given axis of rotation.

Transformation_Commands.name: Shear
desc: Shears the object so that * points on the line stay fixed. * points at distance _d_ from the line are shifted by _d stem:[\cdot] ratio_ in direction of the line (direction of the shift is different for halfplanes with respect to the line). A sheared plane figure maintains its original area.

Transformation_Commands.name: Stretch
desc: The object is stretched *parallel* to the given vector by the ratio given by the *magnitude* of the vector (i.e. points on the line perpendicular to the vector (through its startpoint) stay on their place and distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)
The object is stretched *perpendicular* to the line by the given ratio (i.e. points on the line aren't moved and the distance of other points from the line is multiplied by given ratio.)

Transformation_Commands.name: Translate
desc: Translates the xref:/Geometric_Objects.adoc[geometric object] by the vector.
Translates the vector to the start point.

Algebra_Commands.name: FromBase
desc: Converts given number from given https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix[base] into decimal base. The base must be between 2 and 36. The number must be an integer.
examples: * FromBase("FF", 16) returns 255. * FromBase("100000000", 2) returns 256.

Algebra_Commands.name: ToBase
desc: Converts given number into different https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix[base]. The base must be between _2_ and _36_. The number must be an integer.
examples: * ToBase(255,16) returns "FF". * ToBase(256, 2) returns "100000000".

Financial_Commands.name: FutureValue
desc: Returns the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate. * *<Rate>* Interest rate per period. * *<Number of Periods>* Total number of payment periods in an annuity. * *<Payment>* The amount paid in each period. * *<Present Value (optional)>* Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. If you do not enter a value, it is assumed to be 0. * *<Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
examples: FutureValue(10%/12, 15, -200, 0, 1) yields a future value of 3207.99.

Financial_Commands.name: Payment
desc: Calculates the payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate. * *<Rate>* Interest rate per period. * *<Number of Periods>* Total number of payments for the loan. * *<Present Value>* Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. * *<Future Value (optional)>* A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. * *<Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
examples: Payment(6%/12, 10, 10000, 0,1) yields a monthly payment for a loan of -1022.59.

Financial_Commands.name: Periods
desc: Returns the number of periods for an annuity based on periodic, fixed payments and a fixed interest rate. * *<Rate>* Interest rate per period. * *<Payment>* The amount paid in each period. * *<Present Value>* Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. * *<Future Value (optional)>* A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. * *<Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
examples: Periods(10%/12, -200, -400, 10000) yields a number of payments of 39.98. Periods(10%/12, -200, -400, 10000, 1) yields a number of payments of 39.7.

Financial_Commands.name: PresentValue
desc: Returns the total amount of payments of an investment. * *<Rate>* Interest rate per period. * *<Number of Periods>* Total number of payments for the loan. * *<Payment>* The amount paid in each period. * *<Future Value (optional)>* A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. * *<Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period.
examples: PresentValue(12%/12, 4*12, -100, 5000, 0) yields a present value of 696.06. PresentValue(12%/12, 4*12, -100, 5000, 1) yields a present value of 734.07.

Financial_Commands.name: Rate
desc: Returns the interest rate per period of an annuity. * *<Number of Periods>* Total number of payments for the loan. * *<Payment>* The amount paid in each period. * *<Present Value>* Total amount that a series of future payments is worth now. * *<Future Value (optional)>* A cash balance you want to attain after the last payment. If you do not enter a future value, it is assumed to be 0. * *<Type (optional)>* Indicates when payments are due. If you do not enter a value or you enter 0 the payment is due at the end of the period. If you enter 1 it is due at the beginning of the period. * *<Guess (optional)>* Your guess for what the rate will be.
examples: Rate(5*12, -300, 10000) yields a monthly rate of 0.02 (2%).

Optimization_Commands.name: Maximize
desc: Calculates the free number which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one _local_ maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.

Calculates the position of the point which gives the maximal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local maximum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.
examples: Let _a_ be a slider in [0,10] and _t1_ the right triangle with vertices _C_=(_a_, 0), _A_=(0, 0) and _B_=(0,10 - _a_). Maximize(t1,a) gives 5, the value of _a_ that maximizes the area of _t1_.
Let _c_ be a circle, _C_ a point on it and _D_ a point outside the circle. If _f_ = Segment(C,D) then Maximize(f,C) creates the point on _c_ having maximum distance from _D_.

Optimization_Commands.name: Minimize
desc: Calculates the free number which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The free number must be a slider and the slider interval will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one _local_ minimum in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.

Calculates the position of the point which gives the minimal value of the dependent number. The point must be a point on a path and the path will be used as the search interval. The relationship should be continuous and have only one local minimum point in the interval. If the construction is complicated, this command might return ? to avoid using too much processor time.
examples: Let _a_ be a slider in [0,10] and _t1_ the right triangle with vertices _C_ = (_a_, 0), _A_ = (0, 0) and _B_ = (0,10 - _a_). Minimize(t1, a) gives 0, the value of _a_ that minimizes the area of _t1_ (when the triangle degenerates into a segment).
Let _c_ be a circle, _C_ a point on it and _D_ a point outside the circle. If f = Segment(C,D) then Minimize(f,C) creates the point on _c_ having minimum distance from _D_.
